assessment_items.question_content:
Which of the following is **not** true of the Middle East after World War I?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
In the early 1900s and through WWI, the Arabian Peninsula was nominally under Ottoman rule but was really controlled by various tribal leaders.  Hussein bin Ali, the Sherif of Mecca, was the leading figure in the Hejaz (the Western portion of the Peninsula containing the holy cities of Mecca and Medina) who helped the British against the Ottomans in the Arab Revolt.  He declared himself King of the Hejaz after WWI.

Abdulaziz (known as Ibn Saud) came to power in the Nejd (the central part of the Peninsula including Riyadh in the early 1900s and consolidated his power during WWI (he was not active in the Arab Revolt with the British).  By 1926, he was able to take control of the Hijaz from Hussein bin Ali and declare himself King of Nejd and Hejaz.  This was formally merged into the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia in 1932.

choices.content: Modern-day Jordan and Iraq emerged from WWI as British Mandates (essentially under British control)
Modern-day Lebanon and Syria emerged from WWI as French Mandates (essentially under French control)
Egyptians won their independence from the British in 1922 through a successful revolt
Kuwait didn't get independence from Britain until 1961
Saudi Arabia was created by Hussein bin Ali after he helped the British against the Ottomans in the Hejaz and Palestine

assessment_items.question_content:
True or false:  In January of 1919, King Faisal (son of Hussein bin Ali) agreed to support Jewish settlement in Palestine if, in return, the World Zionist Organization helped the Arabs secure an independent Arab state.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: This is true.

choices.content: True
False

assessment_items.question_content:
What does this map depict?

![Mideast map](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/76b43f4f95c421ea64cb6aa9a8d73a43.jpg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This map shows the general partition agreed upon between Britain and France in the secret 1916 Sykes-Picot Agreement.  It showed French control and/or influence in the Blue areas and area A.  British control and/or influence in the Pink areas and area B.  This secret agreement was counter to the open assurances that the British had made to the Arabs when convincing them to rise up against the Ottomans (the Arabs were expecting an independent state in much of the territory partitioned in Sykes Picot).

In the aftermath of WWI, the territory was partitioned into French and British mandates not too different than those described in Sykes-Picot.

choices.content: State boundaries under Ottoman rule in 1914
1917 agreement between Germany and Russia partitioning the Middle East
Secret Sykes-Picot agreement in 1916 between France and Britain
Map articulated by the Balfour Declaration in 1917
Partitions of the Middle East created by the Treaty of Versailles with the blue area going to the British

assessment_items.question_content:
What is **not** true of the Gallipoli Campaign during World War I?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The Gallipoli Campaign was a debacle for the Allies.  The only redeeming aspect of it was the successful retreat of Allied forces.

choices.content: It was advocated for by Winston Churchill
It was a modest victory for the Allies
It is commemorated in ANZAC day in Australia and New Zealand
It made a hero out of Mustafa Kemal
Despite large losses on both sides, it was generally viewed as a victory for the Ottoman Empire

assessment_items.question_content:
Which of the following gave the Arabs during World War I confidence that the British would give them an independent state that would have comprised the modern-day states of Syria, Iraq, Jordan and Israel after the War.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The McMahon-Hussein Correspondence  gave the Arabs during World War I confidence that the British would give them an independent state.

In the McMahon-Hussein Correspondence was an exchange of letters over 1915 and 1916 in which the British High Commissioner in Egypt, Sir Henry McMahon, reassured the Sherif of Mecca, Hussein bin Ali that the Arabs would have an independent state comprising the Palestine, Mesopotamia and Syria in order to covince them to join the British against the Ottomans.

choices.content: Balfour Declaration
Sykes-Picot Agreement
McMahon-Hussein Correspondence
Treaty of Versailles
Treaty of Sèvres

assessment_items.question_content:
Which of the following is **not** true of the Arabs during and after World War I?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The Balfour Declaration called for British support for a Jewish national home in Palestine.  This caused many in the Arab community to question whether Britain was sincere in its promises made to the Arab leadership prior to the Arab Revolt (which, by most interpretations, included Palestine in a future independent Arab state).

choices.content: They were angered by the Sykes-Picot Treaty between Britain and France that divided much of the Middle East between them
They helped the British fight against the Ottomans in the Palestine Front
The Arab Revolt began in 1916 and was led by the Sharif of Mecca
They embraced the Balfour Declaration because it helped ensure an independent Arab State
They did not get the independent state after the war that was promised in the McMahon-Hussein Correspondence

assessment_items.question_content:
How many estimated deaths were caused by World War I (combining military and civilian deaths)?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: There were roughly 8-10 million military deaths and 6-7 million civilian deaths (it will be impossible to ever have a precise number) so 16 million is a reasonable estimate for total deaths.

choices.content: 2 million
6 million
16 million
60 million
80 million

assessment_items.question_content:
Which country was **not** formed from some part of Austria-Hungary after World War I?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Austria, Hungary and Czechoslovakia came directly from Austria-Hungary.  

Parts of Yugoslavia (Slovenia, Croatia and Bosnia) came from Austria-Hungary.

Lithuania did emerge as a new country after the War, but all of its territory came from the former Russian Empire.

choices.content: Austria
Hungary
Czechoslovakia
Yugoslavia
Lithuania

assessment_items.question_content:
What best described the attitude of the Allies at the Paris Peace Conference after World War I?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Woodrow Wilson had already articulated his Fourteen Points in 1918 and came to the Paris Peace Conference in 1919 trying to promote these ideas.  It was idealistic in that, among other things, it called for no secret treaties, free trade and navigation, self-determination, and the League of Nations to arbitrate between nations to avoid war.  

The British and French, however, had suffered far more at the hands of the Germans and were far more interested in blaming Germany for the War and making them pay for it.
The victorious empires weren't at all interested in decolonization or granting self-determination to groups within their rule.

choices.content: Woodrow Wilson was more idealistic while the British and French were more interested in making Germany pay for the War in resources and territory.
The Allies were eager to work with the Central Powers to promote democracy throughout all of their respective empires.
For fear of causing another war, the Allies worked with the Central Powers to rebuild their respective economies
Britain and France agreed to decolonize their empires but refused to embrace the League of Nations
All of the Allies were eager to exact maximum punishment on the Central Powers

assessment_items.question_content:
Which of these events or facts provides the best justification for blaming Germany for World War I?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: It is completely debatable whether Germany should have been assigned full "war guilt" as the Treaty of Versailles later did.  One could argue that the Austrians were overly aggressive in their ultimatum to the Serbians (and their later declaration of War).  The Russians also began mobilizing in defense of Serbia before Germany had declared war.
The Zimmerman Telegram helped instigate American entry into the War in 1917 but did not trigger the war itself.

Bosnia was annexed by the Austrians.  One could argue that this helped precipitate the assassination of Franz Ferdinand and thus helped trigger the war.  The Germans, however, had nothing to do with this.
The Anglo-German arms race (both on sea and land) helped make the environment ripe for an all-out war between these two powers.  However, the British were as complicit in this as the Germans.

The existence of the Schlieffen Plan by itself did not lead to the war. Germany quickly declared war on Russian and France only days after the Austro-Hungarians declared war on Serbia.  They used Russian mobilization as a pretext, but one could argue that they needlessly escalated what would have been a regional conflict in the Balkans to a pan-European (and eventually much of the world) conflict involving the major powers.  The speed with which they were able to roll into Belgium indicates that they were merely looking for a trigger to execute on their Schlieffen Plan.  

So, the speed and aggression with which the Germans engaged the French and Russians shortly after Austria's declaration of war provides the best justification for blaming Germany for World War I.

choices.content: The Zimmerman Telegram
The annexation of Bosnia
The Anglo-German naval race
The existence of the Schlieffen Plan prior to the War
The speed and aggression with which the Germans engaged the French and Russians shortly after Austria's declaration of war

assessment_items.question_content:
This is the map of Europe in what year?

![Europe Map](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e6927fc01037979abde62bfb60a685b0.gif)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The breakup of the Austro-Hungarian Empire into Czechoslovakia, Austria, Hungary (and parts of other countries) along with the existence of Yugoslavia, Poland and Turkey tell you that this is after World War I (and really after the creation of an independent Turkey from the remains of the Ottoman Empire in 1923).
So that leaves us with 1925 or 1951 as possibilities.
The fact that Germany has not been broken into East and West tells you that this is not after World War II (1951 would have fallen into that category).

assessment_items.question_content:
The Treaty of Versailles between the Allies and Germany at the end of WWI stipulated all of the following **except**:

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The Treaty of Versailles did not call for France to occupy the coal and steel-rich Ruhr region of Germany.  France did eventually do this in 1923 after Germany was unable to continue reparation payments (which added to German humiliation and led more to support extreme political parties like the National Socialists (Nazis)).

choices.content: Payment of reparations in the form of currency and actual resources
French occupation of the Ruhr region of Germany
Demilitarization of all of Germany west of the Rhine and 50km east of it
Shipping coal produced in Germany's Saar region to France
Forbidding German unification with Austria
Limiting German armed forces to 100,000 troops
Assigning "war guilt" to Germany

assessment_items.question_content:
True or false:  With the help of Indian troops in Mesopotamia and Arabs in the Hejaz and Palestine, Allied troops were able to occupy most of the Middle East between the Mediterranean Sea and Persia by the end of World War I.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: This is true.

choices.content: True
False

assessment_items.question_content:
Which of the following was *not* a theme of Woodrow Wilson's Fourteen Points?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The Fourteen Points make no mention of redistributing Germany's colonies and, if anything, suggested a move away from empires altogether.

Woodrow Wilson's fifth (of the fourteen) point was:

*5. A free, open-minded, and absolutely impartial adjustment of all colonial claims, based upon a strict observance of the principle that in determining all such questions of sovereignty the interests of the populations concerned must have equal weight with the equitable claims of the government whose title is to be determined*

This idea was not in-line with having empires at all (empires usually involve control of a region irrespective of the desires of the population).

choices.content: Open treaties (no secret covenants)
Free trade between nations
The idea that people should have a say in who governs them (self determination)
Formation of the League of Nations
Calling for German colonies to be made part of the French and British empires

assessment_items.question_content:
What best describes the Italian Front of World War I?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This front was pretty much a stalemate until Germany began to assist the Austrians in 1917.  They were able to penetrate Italy as far as the Piave River (approaching Venice and other major cities), but the Italians were able to push the Austrians out near the end of the War in 1918.

The Italian Front did divert resources that the Central Powers may have applied to the Western Front, but it did not play a major role in the eventual Allied victory.

choices.content: Italy was able to capture significant Austrian territory from 1915-1917
Germany was able to occupy Rome
Austrians were able to penetrate Italy with German help later in the War, but were never able to knock the Italians out
It played a major role in allowing the Allies to win World War I
Italy was able to secure territory from France

assessment_items.question_content:
What British officer was best known for his role in helping convince the Arabs to rise up against the Ottoman Empire and fought alongside the Arabs during World War I?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: T.E. Lawrence was a British archaeologist in the Middle East who could speak Arabic.  Shortly after the outbreak of war in 1914, he enlisted in the British Army where he was posted to the intelligence staff in Cairo.  By mid-1916, he was working with Arab forces in the Hejaz (west side of the Arabian Peninsula where Mecca and Medina are located) in their revolt against the Ottoman Empire.

choices.content: Winston Churchill
Lord Curbishly
Edmund Allenby
T.E. Lawrence
Thomas Chapman

assessment_items.question_content:
Which group was the victim of ethnic cleansing by the Ottoman Empire in 1915?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: In 1915, the Ottoman Empire placed blame on Armenians for rebelling against the Empire and assisting the Russian Empire (especially after the Ottoman humiliation at Sarikamish in early 1915).  They then used this and the justification of suppressing an Armenian revolt to orchestrate a mass deportation and killing of Armenian civilians.

choices.content: Jews
Armenians
Serbs
Arabs
Bosniaks

assessment_items.question_content:
What was Germany's main justification for unrestricted submarine warfare in World War I?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Britain's blockade of Germany in the North Sea had a large impact on the Central powers ability to import goods, especially food.  Germany had a weaker surface fleet than the British and it was being confined to a corner of the North Sea off their coast.  The only way that they could attempt to retaliate with a blockade of the British Isles was through unrestricted submarine warfare.  The main risk, however, was that this would provoke the United States into entering the War on the side of the Allies.

choices.content: It wanted to goad the United States into entering the war
It wanted to kill as many civilians as possible
Britain's blockade of Germany was crippling and this was the only way to attempt to blockade Britain
Submarines were safer than ships
It was the only way to defeat the Russians

assessment_items.question_content:
Other than Germany's use of unrestricted submarine warfare, what other incident angered Americans and helped catalyze American entry into World War I?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: In the Zimmermann Telegram (January, 1917), Germany outlined to Mexico how it would be likely that the U.S. would enter the War on the side of the Entente as soon as Germany resumed unrestricted submarine warfare (which it planned on doing the next month).  They go on to offer, given the above assumptions, support for the Mexicans to declare war on the United States in the event of American entry into the War.  This telegram was intercepted by British Intelligence and shared with the Americans.  This telegram and Germany's attempt to stir up Mexico against the United States was directly cited in President Wilson's war message to Congress on April 2, 1917.
Germany did not attack any American naval bases nor was its treatment of ethnic minorities of major note during World War I.

Germany did commit atrocities in Belgium at the outbreak of war in 1914 which engendered sympathy for the Entente among Americans, but this did not directly lead to American involvement in the War (which did not occur until 1917).

choices.content: Atrocities committed by Germany in Belgium
German attempt to persuade Mexico to declare war on the United States
German attack on American naval bases in the Pacific
German use of poison gas on the Western Front
Germany's treatment of ethnic minorities

assessment_items.question_content:
What is **not** true about the Zimmerman telegram?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Even though the Zimmerman Telegram tried to convince the Mexicans to declare war on the United States if the U.S. were to enter World War I (arguably to redirect American resources from Europe), the Mexicans didn't take it seriously and never mobilized their troops.

choices.content: It is named after the German Foreign Minister that sent it
It made clear Germany's intention to resume unrestricted submarine warfare
It tried to convince the Mexicans to declare War on the United States if the Americans entered WWI
It caused the Mexicans to mobilize troops on the U.S. border
It angered the Americans and was a major catalyst for U.S. entry into WWI

assessment_items.question_content:
True or False: Passengers on the Lusitania had no idea that it could possibly be attacked by a German u-boat.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: False.  The German embassy, in fact, took a newspaper advertisement out in New York to warn passengers that British ships--military or passenger--might be attacked at sea.

choices.content: true
false

assessment_items.question_content:
True or False:  The British naval blockade of Germany in World War I had only a minor effect on Germany's people and economy.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: False. The British blockade of Germany during World War I is considered to be a key element for the eventual Allied victory in 1918.  It dramatically reduced the Central Powers' ability to import raw materials and munitions. It also caused food rationing and malnutrition among the civilian population of Germany (and Austria-Hungary).

choices.content: true
false

assessment_items.question_content:
Which of the following is **not** true about the sinking of the Lusitania?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The sinking of the Lusitania angered Americans but did not directly lead to American entry into the War.  This did not happen until nearly two years later (in 1917) after the resumption of German unrestricted submarine warfare and the Zimmerman Telegram.

choices.content: Germany took an advertisement warning Americans that British ships could be destroyed
It definitely carried some military cargo
Its sinking in 1915 angered America and Germany stopped unrestricted submarine warfare on passenger liners later that year
It directly caused America to enter World War I against the Germans
It was sunk by a German u-boat off the coast of Ireland as part of a blockade of the British Isles.

assessment_items.question_content:
What type of naval vessel allowed the Germans to not be completely dominated by the British at sea during World War I?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The British had the dominant surface fleet in the world entering into World War I.  Germany's surface fleet was mainly relegated to a corner of the North Sea during the war.  What allowed them to attempt to blockade the British was their use of submarines (U-boats).

choices.content: aircraft carriers
destroyers
submarines
battle cruisers
zepplins

assessment_items.question_content:
Which of the following is **not** true of the Americans' attitude and involvement in World War I in late 1914 and 1915?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Americans were upset about German atrocities in Belgium and were angered by the sinking of the Lusitania.  They also maintained close trade ties with Great Britain.  None of this, however, was enough to convince the United States that it shouldn't be neutral.  Woodrow Wilson would in fact win the 1916 presidential election based on a neutrality platform.

choices.content: They didn't want to get involved in a European conflict
They were eager to come to the defense of their close ally Great Britain
They maintained close economic and trade ties with Great Britain
They were upset about the atrocities committed by Germany in Belgium
They were angered by the sinking of the Lusitania in 1915

assessment_items.question_content:
Which country or empire fought on the side of the Entente in World War I?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Switzerland remained neutral throughout World War I.
The Ottoman Empire and Bulgaria both joined the Central Powers (Germany and Austria) during the war.

Prior to the war, Italy was part of the Triple Alliance with Germany and Austria.  However, when it did enter the War in 1915, it joined on the side of the Entente mainly due to promised territory and historical animosity with the Austrians.

choices.content: the Ottoman Empire
the Austro-Hungarian Empire
Bulgaria
Italy
Switzerland

assessment_items.question_content:
What is **not** an accurate description of Italy immediately before and during World War I?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Italy and Austria had an uncomfortable relationship within the Triple Alliance since Austria had been a historic enemy of Italian unification and it had territory that Italy thought should be part of Italy.

Partially motivated to regain some of that territory, Italy entered World War I in 1915 on the side of the Triple Entente against Austria.

choices.content: Italy was formally a member of the Triple Alliance with Germany and Austria before the War
Italy declared war on France to avenge French seizure of Tunisia
Italy was a historic enemy of Austria  (which had been an enemy of Italian unification in the late 1800s)
Italy was eager to lay claim on territory that was being held by the Austrians
Italy formally joins the Triple Entente in 1915

assessment_items.question_content:
Which was **not** a likely driving factor for Archduke Franz Ferdinand's assassins in Sarajevo in 1914?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
There were aspirations amongst Serbians and other in the Balkans to unify the "Southern Slavic" people into one future state of Greater Serbia or Yugoslavia (which didn't exist at the time).  This was the stated goal of the secret Serbian military society known as the Black Hand and its related youth movement (the assassins were part of this movement, which was called Young Bosnia).

Slovenia and Croatia, however, were already part of the Austro-Hungarian Empire.  On top of that, Austria formally annexed Bosnia in 1908, which only further angered the Yugoslav movement.

choices.content: Anger over Austria's formal annexation of Bosnia
A desire to free Croatia and Bosnia from Austrian rule
Aspirations of forming a unified  "south-slavic"
Anger over Serbia being part of Yugoslavia

assessment_items.question_content:
What is **not** true about the people of Croatia, Bosnia and Serbia?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: They all speak variations of Serbo-Croatian which is a Slavic language and, thus, have strong linguistic ties.  Because of these ties, they are unified into Yugoslavia ("Yugo Slav" literally means "Southern Slav").

The people of Croatia are predominantly Roman Catholic.  Serbians are predominantly Eastern Orthodox.  Bosnia is nearly half Muslim (Bosniaks), but also has large populations of Eastern Orthodox (Bosnian Serb) and Roman Catholic Christians (Bosnian Croat).
They definitely do not have close religious ties.  Their religious division would be a cause of major conflict in the late 20th century.

choices.content: They share close religious ties
They would have their own state of Yugoslavia after World War I
They have strong linguistic ties
Croatians are primarily Roman Catholic and Serbians are primarily Eastern Orthodox
Croatia and Bosnia were part of the Austro-Hungarian Empire during World War I and the Empire overran Serbia during the War.

assessment_items.question_content:
What is **not** generally considered a factor that led to the start of World War I?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The direct cause of the War is generally considered to be the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand, which led to Austria declaring war on Serbia.  This caused Russia to mobilize in Serbia's defense, which gave Germany the pretext for declaring war on Russia and France. Germany's invasion of Belgium on its way to France gave Britain the justification to declare war on Germany.    The domino of events was made possible by the various alliances (most of which were created in the late 1800s) among the powers and the significant arms buildup leading up to 1914.

Tsar Nicholas II was not overthrown in Russia until early 1917 and the Bolsheviks led by Lenin did not come to power until November of that year.  This played a role in Russia exiting the war but did not catalyze the war (which started in 1914).

choices.content: The various alliances among the major powers prior to the start of the War
The arms race between Germany and the United Kingdom leading up to the War
The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria in Sarajevo by groups associated with Serbia
Russian mobilization of its army in defense of Serbia once Austria had declared war
The rise of the Bolsheviks in Imperial Russia

assessment_items.question_content:
Which of the following is **not** true about the major empires leading up to World War I?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
By definition, an empire is variety of states and people (often times with very different languages and religions) that were unified under a common ruler (a king emperor or sultan).

For example, the British Empire at the time included India and much of Africa (both of which themselves have many different ethnic groups and religions).

choices.content: The empires of Europe contained people of many ethnicities and religions
The major powers were in a race to build their empires which added to their wealth and prestige
The United Kingdom had one of the most extensive empires (arguably the most extensive) in large part due to its dominant navy
Germany was a new "empire" and was eager to expand beyond its current holdings in Africa and the Pacific
Most of the empires were unified by their common language and religions

assessment_items.question_content:
What is **not** true of the European empires before World War I?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
It is *not* true that the European powers had little to no influence in Asia and the Pacific. 

The European powers had colonial possession throughout the world, including Asia and the Pacific. The Indian Subcontinent was under the control of the British.  Many of the European powers were competing for influence in China (e.g., British controlled Hong Kong and Germans controlled Tsing Tao in China).  The Dutch and French had significant possessions in Indonesia and South East Asia, respectively.

choices.content: Italy and Germany had each been unified states for less than 50 years
Great Britain, France, Italy and Germany each had possessions in Africa
Germany had a possession in China
The European powers had little to no influence in Asia and the Pacific

assessment_items.question_content:
At what river in France were both Germany's initial drive in the Fall of 1914 and its Spring Offensive in 1918 halted?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: In the Fall of 1914, Germany's offensive was stopped at the Battle of the Marne (First Battle of the Marne).  In Spring Offensive of 1918, they were also stopped at the Marne (Second Battle of the Marne).

choices.content: the Marne
the Seine
Verdun
the Somme
the Aisne

assessment_items.question_content:
What best describes the Schlieffen Plan?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The Schlieffen Plan was Germany's way of dealing with a two front war against the French and the Russians.  Russia had a huge army which would take time to mobilize.  Under this plan, the Germans would use that time to execute a quick and massive invasion to knock out the French (on the Western Front) so that they could then focus on the Russians (on the Eastern Front).

choices.content: German plan to use unrestricted submarine warfare to block the United Kingdom
Austrian plan to annex Serbia
German plan to quickly knock out the French on the Western Front, then a single-front war with the Russians
German plan to help the Austrians invade Serbia

assessment_items.question_content:
This was a seminal battle in 1916 where the strategy of the Germans was to attack French positions from multiple sides and "bleed" the French army into capitulation (The idea being that since France couldn't afford to lose the position, they would throw in every man they had to defend it or capitulate).  Despite over 500,000 deaths on both sides, the French were able to successfully hold their position.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The First Battle of the Marne was in 1914 during the first German push into France.  The Second was in 1918 during the German Spring Offensive.
The Battle of the Somme was another seminal battle in 1916 with huge casualties.  It was mainly between British and German forces.
This description is for the Battle of Verdun.

choices.content: First Battle of the Marne
Second Battle of the Marne
Battle of the Somme
Battle of Verdun
Battle of the Rhine

assessment_items.question_content:
This Treaty in 1918 gave substantial Russian territory to the Central Powers but became void due to the Treaty of Paris .

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: By March of 1918, the Russians were essentially forced to sign the Treaty of Brest-Litovsk which conceded huge swaths of Russian territory to the Central Powers.

choices.content: Treaty of Versailles
Treaty of Sèvres
Treaty of Brest-Litovsk
Treaty of Paris
Treaty of Moscow

assessment_items.question_content:
Russia signed an armistice with the Central Powers to end fighting on the Eastern Front in this December of this year (although hostilities resumed briefly the next year before a formal treaty was signed). 

**In what year did Russia sign this armistice?**

[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints: The Bolsheviks took power in Russia in November of 1917 and were eager to end fighting with the Central Powers and focus on consolidating control domestically.  Because of this, they signed an armistice with the Central Powers in December of 1917 (which was briefly broken in early 1918 as negotiations broke down and the Central Powers captured more territory), eventually leading to the Treaty of Brest-Litovsk in March of 1918.

assessment_items.question_content:
True or false: Austria's reaction to Archduke Franz Ferdinand's assassination in June of 1914 was to place an ultimatum on Serbia to bring everyone involved to justice. Serbia tried to comply, but their efforts were not considered sufficient.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is true.

Austria's reaction to Archduke Franz Ferdinand's assassination in June of 1914 did place an ultimatum on Serbia to bring everyone involved to justice. Serbia tried to comply, but their efforts were not considered sufficient.

Many historians question whether war could have been avoided even if Serbia were able to somehow meet Austria's nearly impossible demands.

choices.content: true
false

assessment_items.question_content:
True or false: Russia's invasion of Austria in defense of Serbia was the justification for Germany declaring war on Russia in 1914. 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The answer is false. Russia did not invade Austria prior to Germany's declaration of war in 1914.  Russia did however begin mobilizing its army in defense of Serbia (after Austria declared war on Serbia). Germany, knowing that it would have to act swiftly in a likely two-front war with both France and Russia, used Russia's mobilization as justification to declare war on it and France.

choices.content: true
false

assessment_items.question_content:
In the Ottoman revolution of 1908, what kinds of changes did the Young Turks hope to bring to the Empire?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The Young Turks were most interested in modernizing the Ottoman Empire along Western lines.  Part of this was minimizing the role of religion in government (prior to the revolution, the Sultan was the absolute head of state and Caliph of Islam).  Beyond that, the Young Turks were also interested in restoring democracy and the constitution.

choices.content: A return to Islamic fundamentalism
A return to strong central rule with limited democracy
A more modern and secular empire
A less nationalistic ideology
Closer ties to the English-speaking world

assessment_items.question_content:
Which statement is **not** true regarding Greek involvement in World War I?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: After the Allied Blockade helps overthrow the King in 1917, Greece officially enters the War on the side of the Allies, not the Central Powers.

choices.content: Greece was officially neutral from 1914 until 1917
King Constantine I leaned towards favoring the Central Powers
Prime Minister Venizelos favored the Allies
Greece eventually enters the War on the side of the Central Powers
The Allied Powers blockade Greece in 1917 which forces the King into exile

assessment_items.question_content:
Which of the following is **not** true about Serbia during World War I?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The Allies were able to mount an offensive against the Central Powers in Serbia in Sept of 1918 to retake Serbia (which was two months before the armistice that ended World War I).

choices.content: The Serbians were able to hold back the initial Austrian invasion in 1914
The Central Powers were able to successfully invade and occupy Serbia in 1915
The Serbian Army was badly equipped relative to the Austrians
Some Serbian forces were able to escape the Central Power invasion and regroup with other Allied forces in Salonica
The Central Powers were able to control Serbia until the end of World War I in November 1918

assessment_items.question_content:
Roughly what percentage of the Serbian military was killed during World War I?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Entering the war, Serbia had a 400,000-man army.  Roughly 60% of them died during the war.

assessment_items.question_content:
What was Woodrow Wilson's winning slogan in the U.S. presidential election of 1916?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: In 1916, the American people, for the most part, were still against entering World War I (despite the fact that the Lusitania was sunk in 1915). Woodrow Wilson capitalized on this by running for reelection with the slogan "He kept us out of war."

choices.content: Make the world safe for democracy
He kept us out of war
Remember the Lusitania
Fourteen points to war
Prosperity and progress

assessment_items.question_content:
What best describes Japan's role in World War I?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
In 1914, Japan was eager to increase its influence in China and the Pacific.  It was already on good terms with the United Kingdom and, in August 1914, declared war on Germany (and then Austria-Hungary).  It soon took possession of Germany's colonies in Asia and the Pacific.

It was in World War II that Japan was allied with the Germans and provoked the United States by attacking Pearl Harbor.

choices.content: It was allied with Germany against the United States
It was the decisive reason why Russia left the war early
It attacked and took over German possessions in China and the Pacific
It provoked the U.S. into World War I by attacking Pearl Harbor
It had several conflicts with the British dominion of Australia
It remained neutral through the War

assessment_items.question_content:
Which is **NOT** true of the dynamics of World War I in early 1918 (January and February)?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
In late 1918, the Bolsheviks had taken control in Russia and were eager to extricate themselves from the War and quickly signed an armistice with the Central Powers.  This allowed Germany to begin redeploying troops to the Western Front (which had been a defensive stalemate for much of the War).  If they could go on the offensive before significant reinforcement from the United States arrived, they had a chance of victory.  On the other hand, it could go the other way if the American troops arrived in time.

However, Russia was out of the war and was not looking to capture territory. The Treaty of Brest-Litovsk in March 1918 actually gave huge chunks of the Russian Empire over to the Central Powers.

choices.content: Russia was looking to capture as much territory as possible before an armistice was signed with the Central Powers.
The Allies were eager for American troops to arrive in significant numbers before Germany had a chance to redeploy troops from the Eastern Front
Germany was planning its Spring Offensive to knock-out the Allies on the Western Front before significant American reinforcement could arrive.
It was unclear whether the Allies or Central Powers would win the War.
Bolshevik Russia had taken itself out of the war and was in negotiations with the Central Powers over the Treaty of Brest-Litovsk.

assessment_items.question_content:
The armistice on the Western Front of World War I was signed on which date.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Armistice Day, which marked the end of fighting on the Western Front (and the end of World War I) was on the 11th hour (11am) of the 11th day of the 11th month (November 11, 1918).

choices.content: October 10, 1918
December 12, 1919
November 11, 1918
October 10, 1917
January 1, 1919

assessment_items.question_content:
Which of the following is true about the European alliances in years before World War I?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
France was allied with Russia in years before World War I. 

In fact, the Franco-Russian Military Convention of 1892 called for military assistance both ways in the event of a third party attack on one of them.  The 1904 Entente Cordial (between the UK and France) and the 1907 Anglo-Russian Convention established good relations between the UK, France and Russia forming the Triple Entente.

choices.content: Great Britain had an alliance with Austria-Hungary
Russia had an alliance with the Ottoman empire
France was allied with Russia
Germany was allied with Great Britain

assessment_items.question_content:
Which best describes fighting on the Eastern and Western Fronts during World War I?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The Western Front was short enough (essentially the northwest corner of France and a bit of Belgium) to make trench warfare feasible.
The Eastern Front, however, was much longer (more than a thousand miles) which could never be practically defended with trenches.
Trench warfare favored the defense.  Because of this, the Western Front devolved into a defensive stalemate while the Eastern Front was much more dynamic and fluid.

choices.content: They were both characterized by trench warfare that gave the advantage to the defense
The Western Front was a defensive stalemate from 1914-1918 while the Eastern Front was much more fluid
The Western Front was much longer than the Eastern Front which made trench warfare impractical
The technology used in the Eastern Front was more advanced than that used in the Western Front
The Western Front was won quickly which allowed for a longer, protracted conflict on the Eastern Front
